title: Chip-carved oak coaster
subtitle: Flat seasoned-oak coaster with a chip-carved geometric border
excerpt: A square oak coaster with a chip-carved diamond-and-triangle border. The chip cut removes small triangular wedges from the surface. Seasoned oak holds a crisp chip-cut edge better than softer woods.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on chip carving. The Artistic Crafts Series: Wood Carving (1898) on geometric surface decoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at a stable moisture content. Dimensionally stable and holds a sharp chip-cut edge cleanly.
A two-stroke cut that removes a triangular wedge: a stop cut driven at 45 degrees, followed by a slicing stroke from the other side meeting it under the chip.
A short cut across the grain that prevents the next pass running beyond the intended boundary.
A short straight-bladed knife; the standard chip-carving tool for triangular and geometric patterns.

content.text: Chip carving removes small triangular wedges from a flat wooden surface to build up a geometric pattern. Each triangle is cut with two strokes: a
stop cut
along one edge and a
chip cut
from the other side meeting under the chip to release it clean.
Seasoned
oak holds a crisp cut corner better than softer wood; pine and lime crumble at the chip corners.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A square of seasoned oak: 10 cm by 10 cm, 8 mm thick. The face must be flat and smooth before marking out. A warped blank makes consistent chip depth impossible. Sand to 120 grit on a flat surface if the face is not already true.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for all cuts; an optional marking knife for the initial stop cuts; a leather strop; Danish oil.
Method

Mark a 2 mm border around the edge as a no-cut margin. Inside it, mark a 10 mm wide chip-carved border. Leave the central 6 cm square smooth. Within the border, mark equilateral triangles: each 10 mm per side.

Drive the stop cut along one edge of the first triangle with the knife tip or marking knife. Hold the blade at 45 degrees to the face, angled toward the center of the triangle. Depth: 3 mm at the triangle center, tapering to zero at the corners.
Make the second stroke with the
sloyd knife

: blade at 45 degrees from the opposite side of the triangle, aimed to meet the first cut 3 mm below the surface. The chip releases cleanly when the two cuts meet precisely. If it does not release, do not lever it; make a third stroke from the third edge.
Work around the full border, completing all triangles on one edge before rotating the coaster 90 degrees.
Clean rough triangle floors with the knife tip in a light slicing stroke.
Sand the flat central face through 180 and 240 grit. Leave the chip-carved border un-sanded: sanding blurs the cut edges.
Finishing

Apply Danish oil to the full surface. Three thin coats with a light 320-grit rub between coats two and three. Danish oil is not food-safe; this coaster is for mugs and glasses, not direct food contact.
Variations
Six-pointed star in the central square: mark a hexagonal grid and chip-carve the alternating triangles.
Round coaster: cut the blank to a circle and lay out the chip pattern on a circular border.
Ash instead of oak: similar hardness and grain behaviour at the chip corners.

items.symptom: Chips tear instead of releasing cleanly
items.cause: The two cuts did not meet at the same depth
items.fix: Drive each stop cut to a consistent measured depth (3 mm) rather than by feel. The depth governs where the second cut must meet it.

items.symptom: Oak splits along the grain during the chip cut
items.cause: Cutting with the grain rather than across it on the short triangle edges
items.fix: Chip cuts should run across the grain. Rotate the coaster so each chip cut crosses the grain direction rather than following it.

items.cause: Both cuts not meeting at the same angle below the surface
items.fix: Both strokes must be at 45 degrees to the face. A shallower second stroke leaves a ridge. Re-cut it more steeply.
